lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market grantmaking foundations and charitable trusts that repeatedly solicit, evaluate, award, administer, and monitor grants for external charitable beneficiaries and have institutional operations separable from a single donor or manager.
lens.excluded_activities: Contract fundraising firms, donor-advised-fund sponsors classified elsewhere, operating charities primarily delivering their own programs, family offices and investment vehicles, pass-through shells, captive corporate giving units, and foundations whose restricted assets or donor-dependent governance cannot support a qualifying control transfer.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Foundations deploy endowed, donated, or trust assets through grants rather than selling services to grantees. Operating resources come from investment income, contributions, and endowment draw, while grants and administration are governed by charitable purpose, payout requirements, boards, donors, and tax rules.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employee occupation and wage mix for broader NAICS 813200 Grantmaking and Giving Services, which includes fundraising and other giving services beyond 813211, rather than task-level AI substitution. Management is 25.33 percent of employment, business and financial operations 30.76 percent, administrative support 16.04 percent, and arts and media 5.68 percent. The range maps research, application intake, summarization, due diligence support, monitoring, reporting, communications, finance, and administration to exposed tasks while retaining human judgment, relationship, fiduciary, and governance work. The broader population's large fundraiser share may overstate exposure for pure foundations, while its functional similarity may still capture much of the workflow.
a.rationale: AI can assist opportunity discovery, proposal intake, eligibility checks, summarization, coding, document review, portfolio research, meeting preparation, monitoring, reporting, knowledge management, communications, and administration. Mission strategy, grantee relationships, contextual diligence, conflict handling, final grant judgment, investment oversight, and board accountability remain human-led.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit task-and-wage study of private, family, community, corporate, and operating foundations that separates investment, program, grant operations, evaluation, finance, legal, communications, and administration would materially change the estimate.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 813200, not six-digit 813211, and includes fundraising-heavy organizations.
Occupation shares do not reveal task exposure or already automated work.
Application summarization is more automatable than mission judgment, relationship diligence, or fiduciary approval.

rho.bridge: TAG reports that 81 percent of staff at grantmaking organizations were experimenting with AI while two-thirds of funders lacked policies or governance frameworks. Candid separately found broad uncertainty around AI-generated applications. Experimentation and applicant-facing policy are translated into five-year implementation of internal exposed workflows; this likely overstates realized labor savings because pilots may remain informal and governance slows sensitive uses, but may understate future use as grant-management products mature.
rho.rationale: Digitized applications, documents, portfolio data, and knowledge work make implementation feasible. Confidential applicant data, bias, explainability, hallucination, legal review, fragmented systems, foundation-specific strategies, board approval, and grantee trust constrain the share that becomes dependable workflow substitution.
rho.change_evidence: A representative foundation survey with verified production use, workflow coverage, time savings, avoided hiring, review accuracy, bias testing, policy adoption, and incident data over multiple grant cycles would materially change the value.
caveats: TAG's membership and technology orientation may overrepresent digitally mature foundations.
Experimentation does not equal scaled workflow implementation or labor savings.
Candid's application survey primarily measures applicant-use policy, not internal automation.

e.rationale: The official industry comprises foundations and charitable trusts that award grants from trust funds or fund selected entities. These are principally mission-governed capital allocators, not recurring outsourced-service firms, and private-foundation assets cannot be diverted to disqualified persons. A very small subset may have separately transferable grant-administration operations or lawful institutional combinations, but most of the dataset's margin-bridged firms are outside the default commercial and control lens.
e.change_evidence: A verified six-digit population file linking legal form, foundation type, endowment and operating revenue, normalized unrestricted operating surplus, donor control, staff operations, service contracts, and transferable control rights would materially change eligibility.
caveats: Grantmaking is capital allocation rather than an outsourced service purchased by grantees.
The margin-bridged n input treats foundation receipts and investment flows as conventional sales and EBITDA.
Donor, trust, tax, and charitable-purpose restrictions sharply limit private acquisition economics.

s5.rationale: Board, trustee, donor-generation, and executive succession occur, and foundations can merge or spend down, but these events usually are not qualifying sales of owner control. IRS self-dealing rules prohibit transfers of foundation income or assets for the benefit of disqualified persons. The range allows rare lawful transfers of eligible service operations or institutional combinations while excluding ordinary governance succession.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census of foundation mergers, conversions, spend-downs, asset transfers, and control changes coded for consideration, successor control, donor restrictions, exempt-purpose continuity, and operating-team retention would materially change the probability.
caveats: Trustee or board turnover is not a qualifying control sale.
No representative denominator of closed foundation control transactions was found.
Foundation mergers and asset transfers remain constrained by governing instruments, tax law, and state oversight.

q.rationale: Grantees do not contractually reprice foundation administration, so implemented savings can remain as operating capacity, lower administrative expense, or additional grantmaking. Retention is discounted because private foundations must make qualifying distributions, boards and stakeholders may redirect savings to grants, compliance and model governance add cost, and the benefit is mission capacity rather than distributable owner profit.
q.change_evidence: Foundation-level before-and-after data linking AI deployment to staff hours, consultant spending, technology and governance cost, administrative-expense ratios, payout, grant volume, and portfolio outcomes would materially change retention.
caveats: Operational retention inside a foundation is not acquirer cash flow.
Mandatory and policy-driven payouts can redirect savings to charitable distributions.
No direct study measures realized AI benefit retention in grantmaking foundations.

d5.bridge: Candid's 2025 Foundation Giving Forecast Survey measures expected and recent grant payments among responding U.S. foundations, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for grantmaking operations. In early 2025, 53.9 percent expected giving to remain about the same, 37.3 percent expected an increase, and 8.8 percent a decrease; median 2024 giving was approximately flat after inflation. This is mapped to a near-flat to modestly growing five-year service-demand ratio, with endowment markets, donor funding, payout rules, policy changes, and community needs creating both directions of error.
d5.rationale: Required distributions, persistent charitable needs, new wealth, and foundation endowments sustain grantmaking. Real activity depends on market values, donations, spend-down strategies, tax policy, government funding changes, and whether larger grants require proportionally more operating service.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year forecast of foundation grant dollars, application and award counts, operating expenses, endowment assets, formations, closures, and spend-downs by foundation type would materially change the multiplier.
caveats: Grant dollars are not constant-quality grantmaking service quantity.
Candid's survey had an 8.3 percent response rate and captures expectations rather than a five-year forecast.
Market performance can move payout dollars without equivalent changes in operating workload.

o.rationale: Mission strategy, fiduciary oversight, conflicts, contextual diligence, grantee relationships, final award decisions, and accountability require a responsible foundation operator. Applicants may self-serve and software can automate intake, screening, summarization, reporting, and portions of monitoring, but tax rules, bias and explainability concerns, and board responsibility preserve substantial operator-required quantity.
o.change_evidence: Comparative data from human-led and AI-assisted grant portfolios covering processing cost, decision consistency, false exclusions, bias, fraud, grantee burden, monitoring quality, and board intervention would materially change the estimate.
caveats: An accountable legal entity can remain necessary even when most processing is automated.
Application self-service does not remove mission, fiduciary, and award accountability.
No source directly measures the future operator-required share of grantmaking quantity.

narrative.executive_view: Grantmaking foundations have a research-, document-, finance-, and administration-heavy workflow that is technically suited to AI assistance. The roll-up lens fit is nevertheless exceptionally weak because foundations allocate restricted charitable capital rather than sell outsourced services, and their assets and governance do not provide conventional transferable owner control.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support proposal intake, eligibility checks, summarization, document review, portfolio research, meeting preparation, knowledge management, monitoring, reporting, and communications. Mission strategy, contextual diligence, relationships, conflict resolution, final grant judgment, investment oversight, and board accountability remain human-led.
narrative.value_capture: Administrative savings can remain within the institution because grantees do not reprice the service. They are likely to become lower expense, added operating capacity, or more grantmaking rather than distributable acquisition cash flow, and governance and compliance costs reduce realization.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied firm count may identify organizations with large receipts, but investment returns, contributions, and grant flows are not conventional sales or EBITDA. Most foundations are donor-, trust-, or board-controlled charitable vehicles and fall outside the default outsourced-service and qualifying-control lens.
narrative.demand_durability: Foundation giving was roughly flat in real terms in 2024 and most surveyed foundations expected stable or higher 2025 giving. Endowment markets, required distributions, policy shifts, community needs, spend-downs, and donor preferences create a wide five-year range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal risks are legal and tax restrictions, donor dependence, non-saleable control, misleading receipts-based sizing, sensitive applicant data, bias and explainability, hallucinated diligence, grantee trust, fragmented systems, market-driven payout volatility, and benefits that accrue to mission rather than owners.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can compress proposal review, research, monitoring, reporting, knowledge, and administrative work across recurring grant cycles.
narrative.principal_weakness: Foundations are restricted capital allocators without conventional saleable ownership, so eligible firms and qualifying control transfers are nearly absent.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 813211 Grantmaking Foundations
sources.supports: Official scope covering grantmaking foundations and charitable trusts that award grants from trust funds or fund selected entities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 813200 Grantmaking and Giving Services
sources.supports: Broader-industry employee occupation and wage mix, including management, business, fundraising, administrative, community-service, and media occupations.

sources.title: Grantmaking in the Age of AI: A Design Studio Report
sources.supports: Reported experimentation with AI among staff at grantmaking organizations and the prevalence of missing funder AI policies and governance frameworks.

sources.title: Where Do Foundations Stand on AI-Generated Grant Proposals?
sources.supports: Foundation survey evidence on AI-generated applications, policy uncertainty, direct-knowledge diligence, and potential use of AI for summarization, categorization, and pre-screening.

sources.title: Self-Dealing by Private Foundations: Use of Foundation Income or Assets
sources.supports: Prohibition on transferring or using private-foundation income or assets for the benefit of disqualified persons and related governance constraints.

sources.title: Taxes on Failure to Distribute Income: Private Foundations
sources.supports: Annual charitable distribution requirement, qualifying distributions, carryforward and set-aside rules, and penalties for undistributed income.

sources.title: More Than a Third of Foundations Expect to Boost Giving in 2025 but Outlook Remains Unpredictable
sources.supports: 2025 Foundation Giving Forecast Survey expectations, response rate, drivers of grantmaking, and inflation-adjusted 2024 giving change.
